candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture be operationalized and distinguished from rival explanations of reform failure? The core open problem is to specify measurable quantities for the 'shadow of the future' among elites versus between elites and the public, and to test whether repeated-game incentives explain resistance better than alternatives such as ideological polarization, principal-agent problems, state capacity limits, constitutional path dependence, or simple preference mismeasurement. Without this, the conjecture risks being too elastic to falsify.

What mechanisms actually convert mass public preference into an iterated coalition that insiders must treat as a repeated-game counterpart? The conjecture predicts reform succeeds when citizens can create frequent, observable, targeted, durable rewards and punishments, but it remains unclear which institutional innovations do this reliably: primaries, recall, referenda, party reforms, transparency rules, civic organizations, media structures, digital coordination, or campaign finance changes. This is a high-value design question because it identifies leverage points rather than merely describing blockage.

Under what boundary conditions do elite repeated games favor reform rather than obstruction? The conjecture emphasizes anti-reform equilibria, but repeated interactions can also stabilize pro-reform bargains when elites expect long-run gains from adaptation, fear systemic legitimacy collapse, or compete to claim credit. The unresolved tension is whether the theory explains resistance in general or only under specific institutional and historical conditions, and what flips the equilibrium from preserving internal bargains to coordinating on change.
